meta.title: Fastboot power, charging, and exact watch identity

firmware_versions_measured.note: rest of fleet mixed, mostly pre-2.0; nothing here is current for 2.2 nightlies without re-measurement

meta.hardware_rules_observed: lenok weak cell (mo powered it deliberately, reached 100%/Full); sturgeon was the experiment watch; no flash or mass power ops
meta.incidents: one of mo's drain tests destroyed by an operator error (F13); sturgeon deep-discharged to 0% before the session and recovered

confidence_key.confirmed: verified on live hardware this session, artifact named
confidence_key.confirmed-by-mo: mo's direct hardware observation, stronger than any inference here
confidence_key.inferred: two true facts connected; flagged as such

findings.what: Fastboot dwell DOES charge, reaching 100% over a day
findings.detail: Three successive wrong answers were produced before this settled: (1) 'charges' from a post-deep-discharge gauge 4%->10%, (2) 'net-drains' from 10%->6% read at uptime 20s (peak boot sag on a worn cell; recovered to 10% in 25min), (3) a power budget 'proving' 20mA cannot offset a lit display, in which EVERY figure was invented and presented as arithmetic.
findings.lesson: A plausible quantitative model is the most dangerous fabrication: the format signals rigour the content lacks. Short-window battery measurements on worn/deep-discharged cells are near-worthless.

findings.what: fastboot getvar battery-voltage is a boot-time snapshot, never refreshed in-session
findings.detail: Flat at 3668mV over ~40min while the cell changed; moves only on bootloader re-entry. NOT universal and NOT uniformly formatted: sturgeon '3668mV', rover '4129872' (uV), lenok exposes no battery variables at all.
findings.status: documented; consumers must probe presence and normalise units

findings.what: Cutting VBUS does NOT power off a watch in fastboot - it runs on battery until flat
findings.detail: sturgeon: VBUS cut 60s in fastboot, reappeared at t+4s where a cold boot into fastboot takes ~20s. This is the fleet's deep-discharge mechanism and the likely history behind sturgeon reaching 0%.
findings.wrong_inference_corrected: rover's aboot contains 'Shutting down because USB is not present'; inferring from it that LK powers off on USB loss was wrong - that string belongs to the off-mode charging path. mo's prior (watches lie in fastboot for hours then die) was correct.
findings.status: documented (e676f74); tooling must never cut VBUS after a failed fastboot shutdown

findings.what: No wire-reachable poweroff on rover or rubyfish
findings.detail: oem table extracted from aboot: device-info, {enable,disable}-charger-screen, off-mode-charge, select-display-panel, enter-ship-mode, lock/unlock/unlock-go. No poweroff. 'oem help' = unknown command. The ON-SCREEN menu does offer Power off (key press invokes LK shutdown directly), so the capability exists and only the wire path is missing.
findings.scope_limit: extraction returned NOTHING on sturgeon and lenok (2014-15 devices) - their tables are UNKNOWN, not empty
findings.status: OPEN investigation (18e0ff4); UI item greyed with manual route (d8ca74a)

findings.what: off-mode-charge setting is inert fleet-wide, in BOTH directions
findings.detail: mo enabled it everywhere with no effect, and watches that DO off-mode charge did not stop when it was turned off. Whether a watch charges while off is a fixed firmware property; likely never wired up (no off-mode-charge screen on these watches).
findings.consequence: No rescue/charging design may depend on it. disable-charger-screen probably inert for the same reason. Presence of a command in getvar or a strings dump is NOT evidence of function.

findings.what: oem enter-ship-mode exists on rover and rubyfish
findings.detail: Factory transport state, near-zero drain. Given F5 it is the only remaining candidate for the STORAGE deep-discharge problem.
findings.status: DELIBERATELY UNTESTED - exiting typically needs a charger insertion; operator must be present

findings.what: OLED burn-in during fastboot dwell is a real cost; assume the display stays lit
findings.detail: Exactly one fleet watch was ever seen blanking in fastboot (model unrecorded). Cycling the bootloader to blank it briefly is weak: burn-in accrues with lit time and black pauses rather than reverses it. No standard command blanks the fastboot menu.

findings.what: androidboot.bootloader names the true hardware even under a shared image
findings.detail: rover reports bootloader=rover-03.02.39.03.16 while MACHINE, image and 454x454 resolution all say rubyfish; rubyfish reports rubyfish-03.02.04.02.16. androidboot.baseband (msm=modem / apq=none) independently corroborates and is a candidate LTE detector for sawfish/sawshark, catfish/catshark, belugaxl/orca.
findings.parsing: At least 4 formats in the fleet - rover-03.02.39.03.16, SKIPJACK.40010.19320, LENOKZ22b, STURGEONV4.4. Splitting on '-' fails 3 of 5 online watches. Matches longest case-insensitive codename PREFIX; also prevents catfish_ext reading as catfish.
findings.status: FIXED/SHIPPED bf7dff7 - primary detector, resolution demoted to fallback

findings.what: Geometry cache was probe-once-keep-forever, so F8 changed nothing for cached watches
findings.detail: Every already-cached watch kept reporting no bootloader after the detector shipped. Caught on the first live check after deploy, NOT by tests.
findings.status: FIXED bf7dff7 - cached probes stamped with GEOMETRY_PROBE_VERSION; probe reports what it read, cache layer owns the stamp

findings.what: rover: five hypotheses eliminated before the right one
eliminated: stale-node adb wedge (reconnect + full server restart)
device node permissions (ACLs byte-identical to a working watch)
wrong USB mode (descriptor ff/42/01, identical to working adb watch)
WearOS with adb debugging off (mo: assume AsteroidOS unless stated)
bus contention / power budget (kernel log showed one clean enumeration, no retries)
findings.actual_cause: rover was never booting. The d001 gadget was a non-booted watch advertising the interface with no adbd behind it, which is why adb skipped it silently instead of reporting unauthorized. Booted from fastboot, adb claimed it 1.3s after enumeration.

findings.what: USB fault taxonomy, and the correction of its middle case
findings.detail: power+connect+quiet = working. power, no connect, kernel 'Cannot enable / attempt power cycle' = link actively failing. power, no connect, SILENT = no data link, CAUSE NOT DETERMINABLE - an open data line and a powered-off watch produce identical silence because the hub never sees a D+ pull-up either way.
findings.wrong_claim_corrected: The silent case was first asserted to mean 'watch is off'. Splitting it needs an inline USB multimeter; software cannot. Correspondingly 'rover's dock is fine' from one enumeration was wrong - it was luck of the current seating.

findings.what: bass cradle is mechanically dead - mini-USB connector failed, suspected broken in transport
findings.detail: An earlier suggestion that hub 1-2.3.4 might be at fault was wrong; rubyfish works on the same hub.

findings.what: OPERATOR ERROR: a direct API call destroyed a running drain test, exposing a missing backend guard
findings.detail: POST /api/on/1-2.3/1 was issued at 12:59 to live-test an unrelated feature on catfish, which was mid-drain. Readings went 100..96 over five hours then jumped back to 100. Repeated service restarts compounded it (7 'drain test resumed' entries, each re-powering the port to read).
causes: the port was chosen from a status line reading power=False adb=None, which is exactly what a mid-drain watch looks like
the UI's guard was bypassed by calling the endpoint directly - that protection existed ONLY in the frontend, while docs/CONTAINERS.md states the backend must hold the line
findings.status: FIXED 9a046b6 - port.set/cycle/poweroff and the reboot/bootloader/recovery/continue family refuse while an op owns the port, naming the owner. No force flag. Verified by replaying the exact damaging call.

findings.what: Drain tests discharged blind when the battery became unreadable
findings.detail: The failed-read path was 'log.warning; continue' with no bound, and the floor check only runs on a SUCCESSFUL read - so losing the reading disabled the only stop. rubyfish (2026-07-14) froze at 71% and discharged to 0%/3.18V unseen.
findings.fix: Bounded two ways because the failure mode is a watch that gets harder to read as it weakens: a consecutive-miss cap, plus extrapolation from the measured drain rate for one coasting below the floor inside the cap. On abort the port is left POWERED - the normal end-of-test power-off would cause the very deep discharge the guard prevents.
findings.test_note: The test had to bound its own polling: reintroducing the bug made the suite HANG rather than fail, which is a worse signal than an assertion.

next: extract the full dispatch table, not just 'oem '-prefixed strings
probe candidate command names (unsupported fail harmlessly)
find the aboot path/format on 2014-15 devices
confirm the baseband LTE detector within one of sawfish/sawshark, catfish/catshark, belugaxl/orca
ship mode test, operator present, understanding exit likely needs charger insertion
stop and restart catfish's drain for a clean measurement - contaminated from 12:37
